content:
# Genesis Chapter 19 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 10v–11r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_019_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_019_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 14–15 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** The two angels came to Sodom in the evening, and Lot was sitting in the gate of Sodom. When Lot saw them, he rose to meet them and bowed himself with his face to the earth

**2** and said, "My lords, please turn aside to your servant's house and spend the night and wash your feet. Then you may rise up early and go on your way." They said, "No; we will spend the night in the town square."

**3** But he pressed them strongly; so they turned aside to him and entered his house. And he made them a feast and baked unleavened bread, and they ate.

**4** But before they lay down, the men of the city, the men of Sodom, both young and old, all the people to the last man, surrounded the house.

**5** And they called to Lot, "Where are the men who came to you tonight? Bring them out to us, that we may know them."

**6** Lot went out to the men at the entrance, shut the door behind him,

**7** and said, "I beg you, my brothers, do not act wickedly.

**8** Behold, I have two daughters who have not known any man. Let me bring them out to you, and do to them as you please. Only do nothing to these men, for they have come under the shelter of my roof."

**9** But they said, "Stand back!" And they said, "This fellow came to sojourn, and he has become the judge! Now we will deal worse with you than with them." Then they pressed hard against the man Lot, and drew near to break the door.

**10** But the men reached out their hands and brought Lot into the house with them and shut the door.

**11** And they struck with blindness the men who were at the entrance of the house, both small and great, so that they wore themselves out groping for the door.

**12** Then the men said to Lot, "Have you anyone else here? Sons-in-law, sons, daughters, or anyone you have in the city — bring them out of the place.

**13** For we are about to destroy this place, because the outcry against its people has become great before the LORD, and the LORD has sent us to destroy it."

**14** So Lot went out and said to his sons-in-law, who were to marry his daughters, "Up! Get out of this place, for the LORD is about to destroy the city." But he seemed to his sons-in-law to be jesting.

**15** As morning dawned, the angels urged Lot, saying, "Up! Take your wife and your two daughters who are here, lest you be swept away in the punishment of the city."

**16** But he lingered. So the men seized him and his wife and his two daughters by the hand, the LORD being merciful to him, and they brought him out and set him outside the city.

**17** And as they brought them out, one said, "Escape for your life. Do not look back or stop anywhere in the valley. Escape to the hills, lest you be swept away."

**18** And Lot said to them, "Oh, no, my lords.

**19** Behold, your servant has found favor in your sight, and you have shown me great kindness in saving my life. But I cannot escape to the hills, lest the disaster overtake me and I die.

**20** Behold, this city is near enough to flee to, and it is a little one. Let me escape there — is it not a little one? — and my life will be saved!"

**21** He said to him, "Behold, I grant you this favor also, that I will not overthrow the city of which you have spoken.

**22** Escape there quickly, for I can do nothing until you arrive there." Therefore the name of the city was called Zoar.

**23** The sun had risen on the earth when Lot came to Zoar.

**24** Then the LORD rained on Sodom and Gomorrah sulfur and fire from the LORD out of heaven.

**25** And he overthrew those cities, and all the valley, and all the inhabitants of the cities, and what grew on the ground.

**26** But Lot's wife, behind him, looked back, and she became a pillar of salt.

**27** And Abraham went early in the morning to the place where he had stood before the LORD.

**28** And he looked down toward Sodom and Gomorrah and toward all the land of the valley, and he looked and, behold, the smoke of the land went up like the smoke of a furnace.

**29** So it was that, when God destroyed the cities of the valley, God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow when he overthrew the cities in which Lot had lived.

**30** Now Lot went up out of Zoar and lived in the hills with his two daughters, for he was afraid to live in Zoar. So he lived in a cave with his two daughters.

**31** And the firstborn said to the younger, "Our father is old, and there is not a man on earth to come in to us after the manner of all the earth.

**32** Come, let us make our father drink wine, and we will lie with him, that we may preserve offspring from our father."

**33** So they made their father drink wine that night. And the firstborn went in and lay with her father. He did not know when she lay down or when she arose.

**34** The next day, the firstborn said to the younger, "Behold, I lay last night with my father. Let us make him drink wine tonight also, and you go in and lie with him, that we may preserve offspring from our father."

**35** So they made their father drink wine that night also. And the younger arose and lay with him, and he did not know when she lay down or when she arose.

**36** Thus both the daughters of Lot became pregnant by their father.

**37** The firstborn bore a son and called his name Moab. He is the father of the Moabites to this day.

**38** The younger also bore a son and called his name Ben-ammi. He is the father of the Ammonites to this day.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 19 spans Folios 10v–11r.

   **Folio 10v** (GEN_019_page_001.jpg): This folio (the verso of Folio 10, which carried Genesis 18) opens with the continuation of Genesis 18 in its upper columns. The Genesis 18–19 transition is visible in the lower portion of this folio, where the two angels' arrival at Sodom is announced — וַיָּבֹאוּ שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים סְדֹמָה ("the two angels came to Sodom"). The text then runs through the hospitality scene (vv. 1–3), the assault by the men of Sodom (vv. 4–11), the angels' warning and instructions (vv. 12–14), and the urgency of flight (vv. 15–22), carrying the narrative through to Lot's arrival at Zoar and approximately verse 22. The three-column Masoretic layout of the Leningrad Codex is clearly legible, with the characteristic square Hebrew script of the Ben Asher tradition. Parasha markers and cantillation marks (te'amim) are visible throughout.

   **Folio 11r** (GEN_019_page_002.jpg): This folio (the recto of Folio 11) opens with the destruction narrative — the sun rising as Lot enters Zoar (v. 23), the LORD raining sulfur and fire (v. 24), the overthrow of the cities (v. 25), Lot's wife looking back (v. 26), Abraham's observation from afar (vv. 27–28), the theological summary of God's remembrance of Abraham (v. 29), Lot's retreat to the cave (v. 30), and the entire cave episode with his daughters (vv. 31–38). The folio continues into the opening of Genesis 20. The names Moab and Ben-ammi (vv. 37–38) are clearly legible at the column's end, as are the etiological formulas "to this day."

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 14 (GEN_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the Genesis 18–19 transition, with Genesis 19 commencing in the text columns of this page after the Genesis 18 material closes. The uncial script of Vaticanus, written in three columns per page in the characteristic 4th-century hand, is legible throughout. Page 15 (GEN_019_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the remainder of the Genesis 19 narrative — the destruction, the cave episode, and the transition into Genesis 20. The chapter markers visible in the right margins ("CAP. XXI." notation for the Vaticanus's sequential chapter enumeration) help orient the material. The Genesis 19 LXX text generally follows the Hebrew closely, with the notable variants documented below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים — "The Two Angels" (v. 1) — From Men to Angels**

The opening word of Genesis 19 makes an identification that Genesis 18 withheld:

וַיָּבֹאוּ שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים סְדֹמָה בָּעֶרֶב
(vayyavo'u shenei hamal'akhim sodomah ba'erev)
"The two angels came to Sodom in the evening."

In Genesis 18:2, the visitors were described as שְׁלֹשָׁה אֲנָשִׁים (sheloshah anashim, "three men"). Now, as the narrative resumes with the two who turned toward Sodom at the end of Genesis 18, they are explicitly מַלְאָכִים (mal'akhim, "angels / messengers"). The Hebrew מַלְאָךְ (mal'akh) means "messenger" — the same word is used both for human messengers and for divine ones; the divine nature here is established by context and by the explicit identification that they were sent by the LORD (v. 13). The formal translation renders "angels" rather than "messengers" because the divine origin of these figures is explicit by verse 13 and because the narrative clearly presents them as supernatural beings who execute divine judgment.

The shift from "three men" in Genesis 18 to "two angels" here resolves the narrative: the third figure, who spoke throughout Genesis 18 as the LORD himself, did not go to Sodom — only the two who were sent ahead. The LORD's own "going down" to investigate Sodom (Genesis 18:21) is thus effected through these two angelic messengers.

The Vaticanus LXX opens with: ἦλθον δὲ οἱ δύο ἄγγελοι εἰς Σοδομα ἑσπέρας (elthon de hoi dyo angeloi eis Sodoma hesperas, "and the two angels came to Sodom in the evening") — reading ἄγγελοι (angeloi, "angels") in exact correspondence to מַלְאָכִים (mal'akhim). The LXX agrees with the MT at this point.

**2. יָדַע — "That We May Know Them" (v. 5) — The Violence of Sodom**

Verse 5 contains one of the most contested words in this entire narrative:

הוֹצֵא אֹתָם אֵלֵינוּ וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם
(hotse' otam elenu venede'ah otam)
"Bring them out to us, that we may know them."

The verb וְנֵדְעָה (venede'ah, "that we may know them") is a Qal cohortative of יָדַע (yada', "to know"). As noted in the Genesis 18 translation notes, יָדַע has a wide semantic range — from cognitive knowledge to covenantal intimacy to sexual intercourse (Genesis 4:1 — "Adam knew Eve his wife"). The question here is whether the men of Sodom use the verb in its sexual sense ("that we may have sexual relations with them") or in a weaker sense ("that we may examine / get acquainted with them").

The interpretive debate has been extensive:

- **The sexual interpretation** (dominant from antiquity through most of the Jewish and Christian interpretive tradition): The context makes the sexual meaning clear. The entire dynamic of the scene — the surrounding of the house by all the men of the city, the demand that Lot surrender his guests, Lot's counter-offer of his daughters ("who have not known any man" — the same verb, v. 8, using yada' in its plainly sexual sense), the violence of the press against Lot at the door, and the angels' use of blindness to prevent the assault — all indicate that the men of Sodom intended sexual assault of the male visitors. The use of the same verb yada' in verse 8 in an unambiguous sexual context (daughters "who have not known any man") confirms that the same word in verse 5 is being used in the same register.

- **The hospitality/interrogation interpretation** (proposed in the 20th century by scholars such as D. S. Bailey): The demand "that we may know them" could mean "that we may examine them / question their credentials" — a reference to the obligation of the city to vet foreign visitors, not necessarily a sexual demand. On this reading, Sodom's sin was inhospitality and xenophobic hostility, not specifically sexual assault.

- **Assessment**: The hospitality/interrogation interpretation fails to account for (a) Lot's extraordinary counter-offer of his daughters, which only makes sense as a response to a sexual demand; (b) the use of the same yada' in the plainly sexual verse 8; (c) the press against the door to "break" it — the violence goes beyond any examination of credentials; and (d) the full weight of the subsequent biblical references to Sodom's sin (Isaiah 1:9–10; 3:9; Jeremiah 23:14; Ezekiel 16:49–50), which, while they emphasize various aspects of Sodom's sin (pride, excess, failure to help the poor), consistently presuppose a grave moral horror that goes beyond mere rudeness to visitors.

The formal translation renders "that we may know them" — using the same English verb "know" that is used in verse 8 for the daughters who "have not known any man." This preserves the exact parallelism of the Hebrew and allows the reader to perceive the same implicit meaning in both verses. It follows the reading of KJV, NASB, and most formal equivalence translations in maintaining "know" and allowing the context to carry the sexual force, rather than rendering "have intercourse with them" (NIV footnote, NLT), which is interpretively correct but foregoes the verbal parallel.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ἵνα συγγενώμεθα αὐτοῖς (hina syngegonometha autois, "that we may have intercourse with them") — using συγγίνομαι (sygginomai), which in Greek is an unambiguous word for sexual union. The LXX translators clearly read יָדַע here in its sexual sense and rendered it explicitly. This is one of the significant LXX interpretive translations that clarifies what the MT's deliberate verbal ambiguity leaves implicit.

**3. לוֹט יָצָא וְסָגַר הַדֶּלֶת — Lot's Counter-Offer (vv. 6–8) — The Moral Horror**

Lot's response to the mob is one of the most morally disturbing passages in Genesis:

הִנֵּה נָא לִי שְׁתֵּי בָנוֹת אֲשֶׁר לֹא יָדְעוּ אִישׁ
אוֹצִיאָה נָּא אֶתְהֶן אֲלֵיכֶם וַעֲשׂוּ לָהֶן כַּטּוֹב בְּעֵינֵיכֶם
(hinneh na li shtei vanot asher lo yade'u ish
otsi'ah na etten aleikhem va'asu lahen kattov be'einekhem)
"Behold, I have two daughters who have not known any man.
Let me bring them out to you, and do to them as you please."

The formal translation renders this exactly as the Hebrew reads. The interpretive tradition has grappled with Lot's offer in various ways:

- The ancient commentary tradition (notably Origen and later rabbinic sources) read Lot's offer as a desperate stalling tactic — he knew or hoped the crowd would refuse his daughters (since their interest was specifically in the male visitors) and thus the offer was a rhetorical diversionary move rather than a genuine willingness to surrender his daughters.
- The more straightforward and disturbing reading: Lot genuinely offers his daughters to the mob to protect his guests — a reflection of the ancient Near Eastern guest-host code, in which the host's obligation to protect guests even superseded the protection of his own household. The guests, having come under his roof, were under his protection (v. 8: "they have come under the shelter of my roof"). The offer is a measure of how absolute the ancient guest-host covenant was — and of how morally catastrophic its application could become.
- The narrative itself neither condemns nor commends Lot's offer. It is reported without editorial commentary. The reader is left to judge — and the subsequent story of Lot in the cave with his daughters (vv. 30–38) creates a bitter narrative irony: the daughters whom Lot offered to the mob to preserve his guests will, in the end, use their father for their own purposes.

The formal translation makes no softening adjustments. The text reads as it stands.

**4. הַפְעַם הַזֹּאת שֹׁפֵט — "This Fellow Came to Sojourn and He Has Become the Judge" (v. 9)**

The mob's response to Lot contains a remarkable accusation:

הָאֶחָד בָּא לָגוּר וַיִּשְׁפֹּט שָׁפוֹט
(ha'echad ba lagur vayyishpot shaphot)
"This fellow came to sojourn, and he has become the judge!"

The accusation turns on Lot's status: he is a ger (גֵּר, "sojourner / resident alien") — a person living in Sodom without full citizen rights. His presumption in telling the men of Sodom what they may or may not do strikes the mob as an outrage. The verb שָׁפַט (shaphot, "to judge") is used in the emphatic absolute infinitive construction (שָׁפוֹט שָׁפוֹט) — "he has been judging / he has made himself judge." The irony cuts in multiple directions: the accusation that a foreign resident is "judging" them echoes back to Abraham's question in Genesis 18:25 — "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?" The true Judge is about to render judgment on these very men.

The formal translation renders "he has become the judge" — capturing the force of the mob's outrage at Lot's perceived presumption.

**5. סַנְוֵרִים — "Blindness" (v. 11) — A Rare Word**

The angels' response to the assault is to strike the crowd with סַנְוֵרִים (sanverim, "blindness"):

וְאֶת הָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר פֶּתַח הַבַּיִת הִכּוּ בַּסַּנְוֵרִים
(ve'et ha'anashim asher petach habbayit hikku bassanverim)
"And they struck with blindness the men who were at the entrance of the house."

The word סַנְוֵרִים (sanverim) occurs only twice in the Hebrew Bible — here and in 2 Kings 6:18, where Elisha prays for the Aramean army to be struck with the same condition. The exact nature of sanverim is debated: it may denote a dazzling blindness (like the effect of a sudden blinding light), a confusion of the senses, or a complete inability to perceive one's surroundings. The result in verse 11 is that they "wore themselves out groping for the door" — suggesting both blindness and perhaps a supernatural disorientation. The formal translation renders "blindness" as the standard English equivalent, noting the word's rarity.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ἀορασίᾳ (aorasia, "sightlessness / blindness") — a word denoting absence of sight. The LXX preserves the same meaning.

**6. יְהוָה הִשְׁמִיד מֵאֵת יְהוָה — "The LORD Rained... From the LORD" (v. 24) — The Double Divine Name**

Verse 24 contains one of the most theologically discussed verses in all of Genesis:

וַיהוָה הִמְטִיר עַל סְדֹם וְעַל עֲמֹרָה גָּפְרִית וָאֵשׁ
מֵאֵת יְהוָה מִן הַשָּׁמָיִם
(vaYHWH himtir al Sodom ve'al Amorah gofrit va'esh
me'et YHWH min hashamayim)
"Then the LORD rained on Sodom and Gomorrah sulfur and fire
from the LORD out of heaven."

The double occurrence of יְהוָה (YHWH) — "the LORD" as the subject who rains, and "from the LORD out of heaven" as the source — has generated extensive interpretive discussion:

- **The Trinitarian reading** (prominent in early Christian exegesis, from Justin Martyr through Origen, and in much of the Christian tradition): The two instances of YHWH refer to two distinct divine Persons — one YHWH on earth executing judgment (the Son / the Angel of the LORD / the pre-Incarnate Christ) and one YHWH in heaven (the Father who sends). This reading uses the verse as one of the key OT "proof texts" for the doctrine of the Trinity and the pre-Incarnate existence of the Son.

- **The Hebrew syntactical reading**: The phrase מֵאֵת יְהוָה מִן הַשָּׁמָיִם (me'et YHWH min hashamayim, "from the LORD out of heaven") is a prepositional phrase specifying the origin of the sulfur and fire — "from before YHWH / from YHWH" — and the repetition is a Hebrew stylistic device emphasizing the divine origin and authority of the judgment. On this reading there is no implication of two separate divine Persons but rather an emphatic double assertion that this catastrophe was entirely of divine origin.

- **The source-critical reading**: Some scholars propose that the verse reflects two originally separate source traditions (one using YHWH as subject, one using YHWH as the heavenly source) that were joined in compilation, producing the double name by editorial accident.

The formal translation renders the verse exactly as the Hebrew presents it — "the LORD rained... from the LORD out of heaven" — without adjudicating among these interpretive options. The double divine name is preserved because it is what the Leningrad Codex reads and because flattening it into a single reference would eliminate the very feature that has made this verse theologically significant throughout the history of interpretation.

The Vaticanus LXX reads: καὶ κύριος ἔβρεξεν ἐπὶ Σοδομα καὶ Γομορρα θεῖον καὶ πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ (kai kyrios ebrexen epi Sodoma kai Gomorra theion kai pyr para kyriou ek tou ouranou, "and the Lord rained on Sodom and Gomorrah sulfur and fire from the Lord out of heaven") — also preserving the double κύριος (kyrios, "Lord"), in full agreement with the MT's double YHWH.

**7. גָּפְרִית וָאֵשׁ — "Sulfur and Fire" (v. 24) — The Nature of the Judgment**

The instruments of judgment are גָּפְרִית וָאֵשׁ (gofrit va'esh, "sulfur and fire"). The word גָּפְרִית (gofrit, "sulfur / brimstone") appears throughout the Hebrew Bible in contexts of divine judgment — notably in Deuteronomy 29:23, Psalm 11:6, Isaiah 34:9, and Ezekiel 38:22. Sulfur (elemental sulfur, brimstone) was associated with volcanic activity and with the burning, uninhabitable quality of certain landscapes in the ancient Near East. The region of the Dead Sea — where Sodom and Gomorrah are traditionally located — is geologically active and contains sulfur deposits, bitumen pits (Genesis 14:10), and evidence of ancient cataclysm in its geography. The formal translation renders "sulfur and fire" — the standard rendering — rather than "brimstone and fire" (KJV), since "brimstone" is an archaism for sulfur that is no longer current in English.

The Vaticanus LXX renders θεῖον καὶ πῦρ (theion kai pyr, "sulfur and fire") — θεῖον (theion) being the standard Greek word for sulfur (with an interesting secondary association: θεῖον also means "divine," making "divine fire" a possible wordplay in the Greek). The formal translation follows MT.

**8. וַתַּבֵּט אִשְׁתּוֹ מֵאַחֲרָיו — "Lot's Wife Looked Back" (v. 26)**

Verse 26 is one of the most haunting single verses in Genesis:

וַתַּבֵּט אִשְׁתּוֹ מֵאַחֲרָיו וַתְּהִי נְצִיב מֶלַח
(vatabbett ishto me'acharav vattehi netsiv melach)
"But Lot's wife, behind him, looked back, and she became a pillar of salt."

Several observations:

- **The verb וַתַּבֵּט (vatabbett, "she looked back"):** The root נָבַט (nabbat) means "to look, to gaze, to observe attentively." The specific form here is a Hiphil feminine singular imperfect — suggesting a deliberate, attentive looking, not a mere inadvertent glance. The command in verse 17 was explicit: "Do not look back" (אַל תַּבִּיט אַחֲרֶיךָ, al tabbitt acharekha). Her looking is a disobedient, deliberate backward gaze toward what she was leaving.

- **"Behind him" (מֵאַחֲרָיו, me'acharav):** This detail places Lot's wife behind Lot in the flight — and she looked back from that position. The narrative does not explain her motivation: longing for her home, concern for her sons-in-law who stayed behind (v. 14), the natural human impulse to witness one's entire world in the moment of its destruction. The text is silent on why. The silence is part of the power.

- **נְצִיב מֶלַח (netsiv melach, "a pillar of salt"):** The word נְצִיב (netsiv) means "pillar, column, monument, post." The specific image is of a standing column of salt — which has been associated with the salt formations of the Dead Sea region. Geological formations in the area near the Dead Sea (particularly the Lisan Peninsula and the salt cliffs of Mount Sodom) do include striking columnar salt formations, and popular tradition identified one of these with Lot's wife. The text does not explain the mechanism — whether the salt pillar is a transformation, a burial under salt-laden precipitation, or a geological entombment. The narrative offers the bald fact: she became one. The New Testament references the episode once — Luke 17:32, "Remember Lot's wife" — as a warning against clinging to what is being left behind.

- **The formal translation renders "a pillar of salt" — the standard rendering, exactly following the Hebrew.**

The Vaticanus LXX reads: καὶ ἐγένετο στήλη ἁλός (kai egeneto stele halos, "and she became a pillar of salt") — στήλη (stele) being the Greek word for a standing stone, monument, or pillar, and ἁλός (halos) being the genitive of ἅλς (hals, "salt"). The LXX is in exact agreement with the MT.

**9. וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת אַבְרָהָם — "God Remembered Abraham" (v. 29) — Zakhár**

The theological hinge of the chapter comes in verse 29:

וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת אַבְרָהָם
(vayyizkor Elohim et Avraham)
"God remembered Abraham."

The verb זָכַר (zakhar, "to remember") in the Hebrew Bible is not a merely cognitive act — divine "remembering" is always effective. When God "remembers" Noah (Genesis 8:1), the flood begins to recede. When God "remembers" Rachel (Genesis 30:22), she conceives. When God "remembers" his covenant (Exodus 2:24; 6:5), the Exodus is set in motion. In each case, divine remembering is the trigger for divine action — it is the turning of the divine attention toward a person or covenant in a way that produces consequences.

Here, God's "remembering" Abraham is the stated reason that Lot was rescued from the destruction of Sodom: God saved Lot not because Lot was righteous in himself (the cave episode that immediately follows raises serious questions about Lot's moral standing) but because God remembered the covenant relationship with Abraham, to whom Lot was bound. The salvation of Lot is thus an extension of the Abrahamic covenant — an act of grace mediated through Abraham's standing before God, precisely consistent with what the intercession of Genesis 18 accomplished.

Note also the divine name: verse 29 uses אֱלֹהִים (Elohim, "God") rather than יְהוָה (YHWH, "the LORD"), consistent with the Elohistic pattern visible in many summary verses throughout Genesis. The formal translation renders "God" where the Hebrew reads Elohim and "the LORD" where it reads YHWH, throughout the chapter.

**10. לֹא יִדַּע בְּשִׁכְבָהּ וּבְקוּמָהּ — "He Did Not Know When She Lay Down or When She Arose" (vv. 33, 35) — The Cave Episode**

The cave episode (vv. 30–38) is one of the most deliberately disturbing narratives in Genesis, reported in the same cool, matter-of-fact narrative style as the rest of the chapter:

וְלֹא יָדַע בְּשִׁכְבָהּ וּבְקוּמָהּ
(velo yada' beshikhbah uvekumah)
"He did not know when she lay down or when she arose."

The phrase appears identically in verses 33 and 35 — a deliberate narrative repetition underscoring that Lot's lack of knowledge is total and that the act is repeated. The same verb יָדַע (yada', "to know") that the men of Sodom used for their intended assault on the angelic visitors (v. 5) is now used — in a different sense but the same root — for Lot's lack of awareness of what his daughters are doing to him. The verbal echo is likely intentional.

The daughters' reasoning (vv. 31–32) is presented sympathetically in its own terms: they believe, apparently sincerely, that there are no men left on earth following the destruction they have witnessed. Their solution is desperate and transgressive — incest with their own father — but the narrative presents it as motivated by the desire to preserve human life ("that we may preserve offspring from our father," v. 32). The text neither condemns nor approves. It reports.

The etiological conclusion (vv. 37–38) makes the narrative purpose clear: the origins of Moab and Ammon — Israel's neighboring nations — are explained through this episode. The names encode the story: מוֹאָב (Mo'av, "Moab") is explained as deriving from מֵאָב (me'av, "from father"); בֶּן עַמִּי (Ben-ammi, "Ben-ammi") means "son of my people / son of my kin." Both names record the incestuous origin of the peoples. Whether these etymologies are linguistically accurate is secondary to their narrative function: they mark Moab and Ammon as kin to Israel (descended from Abraham's nephew Lot) but also as peoples born of the circumstances of Sodom's destruction — survivors of catastrophe, products of moral compromise.

The formal translation renders every element of this passage exactly as the Hebrew presents it, without euphemism.

**11. זְעָר / צֹעַר — Zoar (vv. 20–22) — The Little City**

The name of the city to which Lot flees — צֹעַר (Tso'ar, "Zoar") — is connected in the narrative to the Hebrew word for "small, little" (מִצְעָר, mits'ar). Lot's appeal to the angel (v. 20) uses the word: "Behold, this city is near enough to flee to, and it is a little one (מִצְעָר)" — and then the parenthetical "(is it not a little one?)" (הֲלֹא מִצְעָר הִוא) repeats it. The name Zoar (Tso'ar) derives from the same root צָעַר (tsa'ar, "to be small"), making the city's name a permanent inscription of Lot's plea. The formal translation retains "Zoar" as the place name (the standard English form of the name) and renders the Hebrew dialogue with the word "little" to preserve the wordplay: "is it not a little one?" echoes against the city named "Little."

The Vaticanus LXX transliterates the city name as Σηγωρ (Segor) — a Greek rendering of the Hebrew צֹעַר that differs from the standard English "Zoar." The formal translation follows the conventional English name form "Zoar" (from the MT/Masoretic tradition) rather than the LXX Segor.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 19

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 14–15 (GEN_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_019_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The LXX of Genesis 19 generally follows the MT closely; the following variants are of note:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 19:1 | מַלְאָכִים ("angels/messengers") | ἄγγελοι ("angels") | "angels" | Exact agreement; both texts identify the visitors as angels at the chapter's opening |
| 19:2 | בִּרְחוֹב ("in the town square / street") | ἐν τῇ πλατείᾳ ("in the broad street / square") | "in the town square" | LXX renders the Hebrew rehavim ("broad place, square") as plateia ("broad way / plaza") — close equivalence |
| 19:5 | וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם ("that we may know them") | ἵνα συγγενώμεθα αὐτοῖς ("that we may have intercourse with them") | "that we may know them" | LXX makes explicit the sexual meaning that the MT's yada' implies by context; formal translation preserves MT's verbal form for its parallelism with v. 8 |
| 19:8 | אֲשֶׁר לֹא יָדְעוּ אִישׁ ("who have not known any man") | αἳ οὐκ ἔγνωσαν ἄνδρα ("who have not known a man") | "who have not known any man" | LXX agrees with MT's use of the same "know" verb (ginosko) in exactly parallel sense |
| 19:11 | סַנְוֵרִים ("blindness") | ἀορασίᾳ ("blindness / sightlessness") | "blindness" | LXX uses the rare Greek word aorasia; both texts agree on the striking of blindness |
| 19:14 | חָתָן ("son-in-law / betrothed") | γαμβρούς ("sons-in-law") | "sons-in-law" | The Hebrew chatan can mean either "son-in-law" (married) or "betrothed" (engaged); LXX renders gambrous ("sons-in-law"); the narrative leaves open whether the daughters were married or only promised |
| 19:16 | וַיַּחֲזִיקוּ ("and they seized / took hold of") | ἐκράτησαν ("they seized") | "seized" | Both agree on the forcible taking of Lot's hand; "the LORD being merciful to him" — בְּחֶמְלַת יְהוָה עָלָיו — LXX reads φειδόμενος κύριος αὐτοῦ ("the Lord sparing him"), rendering chemlah ("pity, compassion") as pheidomai ("to spare") |
| 19:22 | צֹעַר ("Zoar") | Σηγωρ ("Segor") | "Zoar" | Different transliterations of the same place name; formal translation follows MT convention |
| 19:24 | יְהוָה... מֵאֵת יְהוָה ("the LORD... from the LORD") | κύριος... παρὰ κυρίου ("the Lord... from the Lord") | "the LORD... from the LORD" | Both MT and LXX preserve the double divine name; the LXX uses kyrios for both, in full agreement with MT's double YHWH |
| 19:26 | נְצִיב מֶלַח ("a pillar of salt") | στήλη ἁλός ("a pillar/stele of salt") | "a pillar of salt" | Exact equivalence |
| 19:29 | וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים ("God remembered") | ἐμνήσθη ὁ θεός ("God remembered") | "God remembered" | LXX renders Elohim as ho theos ("God") and uses mimneskomai ("to remember") in the same effective sense |
| 19:37 | מוֹאָב ("Moab") | Μωαβ ("Moab") | "Moab" | Exact transliteration |
| 19:38 | בֶּן עַמִּי ("Ben-ammi") | Αμμαν ("Amman") | "Ben-ammi" | LXX uses the later place-name form Amman rather than the narrative personal name Ben-ammi; formal translation follows MT's personal name form |

The most significant LXX variant in Genesis 19 is the explicit sexual rendering of יָדַע (yada') in verse 5 — ἵνα συγγενώμεθα αὐτοῖς (hina syngegonometha autois, "that we may have intercourse with them"). The LXX translators made an unambiguous interpretive decision here, removing the ambiguity that the MT's use of the general word "know" preserves. The formal translation retains the MT's verbal form.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 19 — Six Scenes**

Genesis 19 divides into six scenes, forming a movement from hospitality and threat through destruction to its terrible aftermath:

**Scene 1 — Lot's Hospitality at the Gate (vv. 1–3):** The angels arrive at the gate of Sodom. Lot, like Abraham in Genesis 18, sees them, rises to meet them, bows, and offers hospitality. The parallel is deliberate and precise: the same verbs, the same gestures, the same unleavened bread baked for guests. Lot's hospitality mirrors Abraham's. The scene establishes Lot as a righteous host — the one genuinely hospitable person within the wicked city.

**Scene 2 — The Assault by the Men of Sodom (vv. 4–11):** All the men of Sodom, young and old, surround the house and demand the visitors for sexual violence. Lot's extraordinary counter-offer of his daughters. The escalating violence. The angels' intervention with blindness. The scene defines Sodom's sin with brutal clarity: the utter failure of hospitality and the inversion of the guest-host covenant — where guests should be protected, Sodom seeks to violate them; where strangers should be welcomed, Sodom surrounds and threatens.

**Scene 3 — The Warning and the Flight (vv. 12–22):** The angels' revelation of their mission (v. 13: "we are about to destroy this place"). Lot's warning to his sons-in-law, who laugh it off. The angels' urgency as dawn breaks. Lot's lingering — and the mercy of the angels who physically seize his family and drag them out. Lot's negotiation for Zoar. The scene is marked by the remarkable phrase in verse 16: "the LORD being merciful to him" — the flight from Sodom is not merely escape, it is an act of divine grace.

**Scene 4 — The Destruction (vv. 23–25):** The sun rises as Lot arrives at Zoar. The LORD rains sulfur and fire on Sodom and Gomorrah. The entire valley is overthrown. The brevity of this scene is remarkable — four verses for an event of catastrophic magnitude. The narrative does not linger. There is no description of the screaming, no account of the deaths, no inventory of the destroyed. The destruction is stated, not described. The restraint is its own horror.

**Scene 5 — Lot's Wife and Abraham's View (vv. 26–29):** The single verse about Lot's wife (v. 26) stands as a monument: one sentence, one woman, one backward glance, and she is gone. Abraham's morning observation of the smoke from afar (vv. 27–28) — he went early to the place where he had stood before the LORD and looked out over the destruction he had hoped to prevent. The theological summary of verse 29 brings the Abraham thread back: God remembered Abraham.

**Scene 6 — Lot and His Daughters in the Cave (vv. 30–38):** The final scene is the darkest. Lot, having lost everything, retreats to a cave. His daughters, believing all men on earth are dead, use their incapacitated father to perpetuate their line. The two resulting sons — Moab and Ben-ammi — are the ancestors of Israel's perennial neighbors, the Moabites and the Ammonites.

**Sodom's Sin — The Biblical Witness**

The nature of Sodom's sin has been debated extensively in both Jewish and Christian tradition. The biblical witnesses beyond Genesis 19 are worth noting:

- **Ezekiel 16:49–50** provides the most explicit prophetic summary: "Behold, this was the guilt of your sister Sodom: she and her daughters had pride, excess of food, and prosperous ease, but did not aid the poor and needy. They were haughty and did an abomination before me." Ezekiel identifies Sodom's sin as primarily: (1) pride, (2) excess and prosperous complacency, (3) failure to help the vulnerable, and (4) an "abomination" (תּוֹעֵבָה, to'evah). The term "abomination" in verse 50 is left unspecified — it likely refers to the specific act of Genesis 19:5, but the primary indictment in Ezekiel emphasizes the social and structural sins before the sexual violation.
- **Isaiah 1:10; 3:9**: Isaiah uses "Sodom" as a byword for radical wickedness and shameless sin.
- **Jeremiah 23:14**: "In the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen a horrible thing: they commit adultery and walk in lies; they strengthen the hands of evildoers... they have all become like Sodom to me."
- **Jude 7** (New Testament): "...just as Sodom and Gomorrah and the surrounding cities, which likewise indulged in sexual immorality and pursued unnatural desire (ἑτέρας σαρκός, 'other flesh'), serve as an example by undergoing a punishment of eternal fire." Jude explicitly connects Sodom's sin with sexual immorality and "other flesh" (frequently interpreted as a reference to same-sex acts, though some interpreters read ἑτέρας σαρκός as referring to the attempt to violate angels — beings of a different flesh than human).

The formal translation presents the text of Genesis 19 without theological adjudication of these subsequent interpretations. The text of Genesis 19 itself is unambiguous in presenting the men of Sodom as intending sexual assault on the angelic visitors; the broader question of whether Sodom's primary guilt was sexual or social is a matter on which the prophetic witnesses offer a range of emphases.

**The Lot Narrative and Abraham — Irony and Grace**

The placement of the Lot cave episode (vv. 30–38) immediately after the declaration "God remembered Abraham" (v. 29) creates a profound narrative irony. Lot, saved for Abraham's sake, ends his story in a cave, drunk, unwittingly fathering the children of his own daughters. The man who offered his daughters to a mob to protect his guests is now unable to protect even himself from those daughters' purposes. The narrative does not moralize. It simply presents the wreckage.

Yet within this wreckage, the larger narrative of redemption continues: Ruth — the greatest Moabitess in the Hebrew Bible — is a descendant of these unions (Ruth 1:4; 4:10), and through her comes David (Ruth 4:17–22) and, in the New Testament genealogy (Matthew 1:5), Jesus. The son of Lot becomes the grandfather of Ruth becomes the ancestor of the Messiah. The biblical narrative is not embarrassed by these origins; it traces them with matter-of-fact precision and allows the reader to see that the purposes of God move through the most broken and compromised of human circumstances.

**"The LORD Being Merciful to Him" (v. 16) — Chemlah**

The brief parenthetical in verse 16 is theologically significant:

בְּחֶמְלַת יְהוָה עָלָיו
(bechemlat YHWH alav)
"the LORD being merciful to him"

The word חֶמְלָה (chemlah, "compassion, pity, mercy") describes the LORD's motivation for the angels' physical intervention in Lot's paralysis at the gate. Lot lingered — he could not bring himself to move — and the angels physically seized him and his family and dragged them out. The explanation offered is divine mercy: Lot did not flee because of his own righteousness or wisdom; he fled because the LORD pitied him and acted directly. This is a moment of pure grace — undeserved intervention to save a man who is incapable of saving himself.
